terms.definition: indestructible; not capable of being destroyed. The soul cannot undergo the transformation called death because it was never born into the category of things that can die.
terms.roots: from naś (to perish) with privative a-

terms.definition: pervaded, spread throughout, extended across; from √tan (to stretch, extend). Consciousness stretches to include — yet never merges with — what it illumines.
inexhaustible, without depletion; not subject to diminution or expenditure. Unlike prakṛtis transformations which expend their guṇas, the soul is not diminished by what it witnesses.
terms.roots: from vi (apart) + i (to go) with privative a-

terms.definition: know it, understand it. The grammar of instruction here is epistemological: this is not revealed truth to be accepted blindly, but observable truth to be recognized.
is capable of, has the capacity to; root √arh (to merit, deserve, be worthy). Absolute impossibility: na kaścit kartum arhati — no one can [do this].
not disturbed, undisturbed; lit. un-struck, un-violated — in Sāṅkhya terms, the puruṣa (conscious witness) remains unaffected by the perpetual injections of prakṛtic activity
terms.roots: from pāpa (strike) + viddha (struck) with privative a-

terms.definition: being filled continuously — the ocean's volume is maintained by ceaseless input. Unlike the kāmī (desirer) who empties with indulgence, the devotee receives without depletion
steady foundation, immovable base — acala (unmoving) + pratiṣṭha (standing firm). The root access of consciousness that no prakṛtic process can revoke
ocean, gathering-place of waters — from sam (together) + udra (water). The field containing all phenomena without being modified by them
mastery, control — from vaś (to be subject to). Not the will-to-power of forceful domination, but the effortless sovereignty of the full
terms.roots: from vaś (to wish, to be subject to) + kāra (making)

terms.definition: peace, tranquility, cessation of agitation — not the absence of stimulus but the presence of completion. The goal of Sāṅkhya and the fruit of yoga
you must tolerate/endure — active forbearance, not passive suffering; the austere patience that maintains practice through all conditions
sense-object contacts — the interface where consciousness meets prakṛti; the API boundary where input triggers response
impermanent, non-eternal — the defining quality of all prakṛtic phenomena; that which cannot be relied upon for lasting satisfaction
those that come and go — describing sensations as temporary visitors, not permanent residents
cold and heat — the classic paired opposites; all dvandva that disturb the undiscriminating mind
O son of Kuntī — appellation invoking Arjuna's maternal lineage; reminder of the duty that comes from noble birth
O descendant of Bharata — appellation invoking Arjuna's paternal lineage; reminder of the greatness of the Bharata dynasty
